I remember reading once that Enlightenment is just the space between thoughts. I think the same is true of finding God between the intervals in music.

So we should foster Eden in our hearts and minds as much as we should in our homes. 

Replace the noise with silence, a form of music. 

Allow the cicadas and birds to harmonize, allow the melodies of life to glisten.

Become a sacred space in which God can dwell.

It has once been said that modern man cannot find God because he does not look low enough.

I would add that he cannot hear God because of the static... both external and internal. 

To empty oneself is to allow the silence of God to speak.

How they've stolen silence from us, the very medium in which God speaks!

Our night skies, commutes, walks, chores, and daily existence has been consumed by the noise...

We cannot hear ourselves think. All has been touched by the static.

core.text:
All too often, men will live in perpetual, passive fantasy, temporarily fulfilling their masculine responsibilities online until the façade wears off, prompting an escape and return to fantasy.

Video games, of course, facilitate this. At the expense of their own responsibilities, men take on virtual ones, maxing out their Elden Ring stats and pouring hours into video games that are truly quite difficult.

Consequently, the very real, physical man with the controller in his hand becomes weak and soft while his virtual avatar grows stronger and stronger. 

Thus, they live a shadow life they are too timid to live in reality, completely blind to the fact that they are unconsciously living out their fantasy to be their own knight (the archetype of the hero) in reality, who will one day save the princess in distress (their anima).

Courage, then, is the only solution. 

Men project their own courage and heroism onto Link, which they are consciously unaware of. But every hero one day suddenly realizes he is apart of a strange, divine story, one destined for greatness. To reclaim this shadow project is to realize one's one heroism.

Not attractive, but rather a hypertrophied symbolic representation of fertility.

When you pray to something, and that thing is emblematic of something, it becomes almost a caricature.

In short, we STILL think a fat ass and wide hips are attractive, but the fertility doll you pictured was not a representation of what that culture found attractive, but rather an object of worship in order to facilitate fertility.

Yes, but that quote has nothing to do with a comparison between the cathedrals of the Middle Ages and the technological machines we’ve created.

It sort of misses the point of cathedrals entirely.

You can know God through study (thought) or you can know him through the spirit (feeling).

Cathedrals facilitate the latter, while the technological machines facilitate the former, except considering the very real fact that 90% of modern scientists either don’t believe in God or believe humans are God, both erroneous and prideful positions Einstein wouldn’t agree with.

Idk if that’s u cause I don’t know u but the comparison between technological machines and cathedrals reaching for the divine through the spirit of beauty is out of touch and honestly quite offense for the religious or artistically minded.

Say hello to the technocrats!

The first step to seize control is through art. All authoritarian societies have done so.

This is why A.I. developers were (and are) so focused on generative art as opposed to mundane tasks (like they said).

They would hold not only the keys to culture, but the keys to everything, as everyone is being duped into implementing this “phd in their pocket” into every damned thing possible.

And they’d be the only ones that understood how it worked. 

Instead of artistocrats we’d have dystopian technocrats.

This perfectly encapsulates the novel, narcissistic “techno-religion” I’ve noticed has permeated mostly thinking-sensate types.

We built cathedrals to humbly honor God and reflect his beauty (although we always fail). We’ve built computers to try and become God, for if we supposedly get advanced enough, we can create “consciousness” or sentient A.I. (a fruitless task).

It is, of course, pride, that we can create life itself. Instead of honoring God we’ve killed him. How murderous are we humans, puffed up with pride?

After some thinking, I've come to the conclusion that a lot of mental health issues come from not living up to our values and beliefs. 

We often say we value or uphold some quality or characteristic, but we also unconsciously act in opposition to these values.

The pain of believing in something and not acting in accordance with it causes incredible friction, leading to a sort of generalized anxiety that can develop into something quite serious and gruesome if one does not pay attention...

Pro-natalists are, of course, adults who want to be parents - that is all.

And this is what the anti-natalist argument does not understand: it is not about the quantitative amount of children on the Earth, but rather the shared parent-child relationship, passing one’s genes (a part of their soul) onward and forward through life.

A refusal to do this is an unconscious inferiority complex, as it is obviously both a biological and spiritual desire to procreate and thus pass on your genetics (soul) to your child. 

Those who do not want to do this usually feel a sense of inferiority which they mask under the guise of being compassionate or ideologies such as “anti-natalism”.

In short, the rhetoric around natalism (especially anti-natalism) is incredibly emotionally charged, but the emotions are guises under the veneer of rationality and compassion.

The child, according to Carl Jung, is a psychological symbol of rebirth and renewal. The child is the god Pan (archetypally represented in Peter Pan), representing infinite possibilities and serves as a sort of guide for those who age and thus leave “Neverland”.

Those who see no benefit in having children have already died, for the child within has no room to express itself. Thus, anti-natalism is anti-life, for they make an “improper sacrifice”, much like Cain in the story of Cain and Abel. They think their sacrifice is pleasing when in reality it is revolting.

Fate is for those who forgot how to chart their own course through life, bumbling and timid in the face of action as they are dragged by their heels.

Destiny is reserved for the individual who recognizes their purpose in life which was ordained by God. They understand the meaning of synchronicities and make the courageous decision to become the man they always knew they could - which was God’s plan all along.

To me it’s quite simple: evolution was the mechanism in which God used to create the earth.

God is an artist, and every sculpture was once solid marble.

Over time and with careful deliberation, God created life through nature, ultimately creating us “from dust” - that primordial time on Earth before the seas, the trees, the animals and the plants.

Evolution has the same claim: that life was created from dust, essentially… that the earth was once barren and over time blossomed into life over evolution.

Evolution is one of the many brushes God has used to create the cosmos, and He is a patient and careful artist.

For this topic (regarding the archetype of the hero), Joseph Campbell’s “A Hero with a Thousand Faces” is a classic. It is where we get the popular term “hero arch” in regard to anime or media in general, but the book predominately refers to the hero arch in relation to our psychology like I touched upon.

Another great book is “The Problem of the Puer” by Marie Louise von Franz. That one blew me out of the water.

It’s quite simple: cultures are a living habitat. 

While influence from neighboring habitats is normal and healthy, this is usually a very long process. This way, adaptation can occur.

However, if the habitat is introduced to an organism that is too different, it can completely disrupt the ecosystem, ravaging the beautiful trees, animals, and plants that took thousands of years to develop (these would the be cultural artifacts & ideas). 

The best example is, of course, the West’s discovery of the New World. The metaphor works in regard to culture but also its literal meaning as well, as the natives could not adapt to the foreign pathogen).

The left has forgotten anthropology. 

Culture is a living spirit: it is literally alive. We keep the memory of our ancestors alive by using their language and continuing their customs. What once was grandma’s recipe, over time, becomes a national dish. 

The heroic effort of an individual can grant them their last name. It is why my last name is Barrera, for my Asturian ancestors fought off the Moors: they were the “barrier” of Spain, and their heroic efforts lives through the cultural artifact of my last name. They live through me eternally, both in name and my culture.

When you disrupt this, you murder the living spirit of the ancestors which is forever embedded in culture.

Tolkien shared such a view. 

Culture is wonderful, and the Left’s insistence that we are all the same (DEI) spits in the face of their fake love for all “people of color”.

We are all different, and because of this we grow.

The Left, who insist that we can easily throw Hatians in rural Ohio are the same who abhors the introduction of invasive Cane Toads in Australia. While obviously humans are to be treated with compassion and not like animals, nevertheless the effect on the cultural ecosystem is the same.

When you understand culture is alive in a similar way to ecosystems, you understand the insanity of their propositions.

~ an anthropologist

It is no wonder, then, why many “pseudo”-intellectuals attempt to escape the devouring mother through perpetually asking “why”, thriving in a world of high thought and abstraction - it is a world that mommy cannot understand.

Thus, like a child, many with a devouring mother complex continue asking “why” - looking for a revelation or new framework that will free them from their suffocation - instead of taking action.

They don’t take action because unconsciously they would like to remain as a child - unbounded and irresponsible.

They abandon the Tao, which cannot be understood, for the moment it is conscious it is no longer the Tao that works through us.

Every intellectual and academic falls to their knees in horror at the sight of the child who never stops asking “why”.

The child knows the game - he asks “why” precisely because he knows, at one point, the adult will have to concede and say: “because of the way it is, now shut up (and stop dismantling my own notion of understanding)”.

And thus the child wins, crumbling the tower of pride the intellectual covets:

“Truly, I say to you, unless you turn and become like children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Whoever humbles himself like this child, he is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven.” ~ The Book of Matthew 

However, the intellectual & the child share this in common: they both continue to ask why, sometimes at their detriment.

The former must balance his search of knowledge with the understanding that he himself is incapable of truly “knowing”, but that the pursuit is noble if pride is slain.

Evil is discord and as a consequence cannot be integrated, as when we are in harmony we are made whole.

Music is whole only when there is harmony in melody & in rhythm - a musician who plays the wrong notes is ousted from the band. He cannot be integrated, as he disrupts the harmony & wholeness of the song. 

"One becomes two, two becomes three, and out of the third comes the One as the fourth" is the alchemical axiom that C.G. Jung uses, in part, to justify his quaternity that integrates evil into the Godhead. 

Yet, this is a misinterpretation, as the axiom conveys the trinity as opposed to a quaternity:

Take three separate musicians, for instance. One is a violinist, the second a pianist, and the third a vocalist. In unison, these three musicians perform in harmony to produce "the One as the fourth": the song itself, with its perfect harmony and wholeness.

Thus, Christ, God, and The Holy Ghost sing in unison to produce the trinity - "the One as the fourth". Introduction evil into the trinity results in discord and thus it is broken, lacking wholeness.

The alchemists were Christian, after all.

The coming of Christ is heralded by the sound of trumpets.

God spoke the world into existence - I imagine He sang, as did Tolkien.

Some imagine language to be the foundation of existence, especially those with an understanding of programming.

But rather, it is frequency and sound - music itself. Music is the only universal language, one we all intuitively understand. Language obfuscates, while music consolidates with its wonderful harmony and organic rhythm.

Do our hearts not beat to a tempo? Do the Cicadas not hum and dance?

Life would not only be a mistake without music, as Nietzsche put it, but it would be impossible.

Music is meaning itself.

No philosophy, no system, no theory, no academic treatise, and no ideology has helped me find solace or God eternal;

It has always been music:

“And it came to pass that Ilúvatar called together all the Ainur and declared to them a mighty theme, unfolding to them things greater and more wonderful than he had yet revealed; and the glory of its beginning and the splendour of its end amazed the Ainur, so that they bowed before and were silent.”

~ J.R.R. Tolkien, Ainulindalë

Integrating the shadow is a tricky concept.

Our shadow self operates unconsciously, hence why it casts a shadow off of our light (which is consciousness).

It only makes sense, then, when we look at our shadow with our light (conscious awareness), it is integrated not as a shadow but as light, for we are aware of it. The shadow dissipates in the presence of light.

This allows us to broaden our capacity to love. We don’t look at our shadow because we do not love aspects of our self. And to love our neighbor we must love ourself.

So, it is “integrated”, but that wording has certain spiritual implications that can be worrying (ie the necessity of evil to balance good - a Manichaean concept).

For shadow contents are only in the shadow because they are neglected for whatever reason. These attributes become “devilish”, when if they were in the light of consciousness, we could decide whether to use ourselves for good or for evil.

But the shadow is unconscious, so it does what it wants, for ill or for good, so it must be faced. But do we integrate evil, or bring it to light?

The conception of the shadow, beginning in the works of C.G. Jung (but has been referred to under many different names in the past) doesn’t necessarily agree with what you say.

Bringing light to the shadow self is the fundamental notion of “shadow work”. We analyze dreams, affects, fantasies, and other unconscious phenomena in order to understand our “unconscious”. When we understand something, we are aware of it. Hence, it is conscious and brought to light.

Being “in touch with the shadow self” without bringing it to light or awareness is a paradox, as once you touch something - ie make contact with - it is within your grasp: conscious and thus not a shadow.

And, the “shadow self” is an alliteration, a metaphor if you will. It is always here and always an aspect of us. We are of course one thing and one mind. It is not a literal thing.

But we need to explore our unconscious, our “palace of memory” as St. Augustine puts it, so our shadow self doesn’t take the rains.

Jung is a conflicting character to me. 

He says absolutely profound things, such as:

"I repeat, he who's heart has not been broken over the Lord Jesus Christ drags a pagan within him that holds him from his best". 

How badass is that?

But, primarily, he counteracts Jung's quaternary model of the psyche. 

In Jungian psychology, Jung states we have 4 psychological types: "thinking & feeling (rational types) and intuition and sensation (irrational types). 

This makes sense, but he derives this, in part, from the trinitarian Godhead. 

In Jung's Aion & Answer to Job, he purports that in order for God to be "whole", Satan must be integrated into the Godhead, turning the trinity into a quaternity: God, Holy Spirit, Christ, & Satan. 

Jung see's Satan as Christ's shadow. He uses the story of Jesus' temptation in the desert as Christ "cutting Himself from His own shadow: Satan". 

Because Christ cut Himself from His shadow, Jung suggests that God must "re-incarnate his evil self as the anti-Christ". 

Yikes. 

Jung rejects privatio boni, which is the dogma of the privation of good, the evil is the abscence of good.

At the root of Jung's psychology lies a religion. It's a topic I've been working on for a while. A lot of it is convulted for sure. 

I think Jung was duped. He thought he was studying psychology, but in reality he was starting a mystery religion.

But it's important we grapple with Jung's work, for he is by far one of the most influential spiritual thinkers of the 20th century.

This is an incredibly interesting question that cannot easily be answered. 

In short, Jung saw in the pages of the alchemists his "process of individuation". He saw the alchemists and their attempt to find the philosophers stone - among other ventures - as a projection of the inner search for "the Self": the central ordering archetype according to Jung. 

It makes sense. Alchemical work is inherently introverted, so as opposed to finding God externally, they try to find Him internally ("as without, so within" being a common precept).

This is not without its merit. It is true - God speaks from within us, all of us carry a divine spark, and we were made "in the image of God" according to Genesis.

But the problem is becoming enveloped in the a world of images, the "symbolic realm" if you will. It can lead to a number of mistakes. It is why for this reason alchemy is often lumped in with the occult and other scrupulous practices (which I think is, in part, disingenuous, as the alchemists were still Christian nonetheless - it's just they swam in similar waters). 

The main question one should ask themselves when searching for "the Self" is this: why?. 

It is why I have become disillusioned with many of the core tenants of Jung's psychology, namely individuation (although that is not to say I am an antagonist of Jung; to him, I owe him dearly for opening up the realm of the irrational among, other things, but I digress). 

In Buddhism, there is something known as a "tulpa". A tulpa is, essentially, an ethereal creature that we create with our own consciousness. That is, it seems or appears real, but it is simply an apparition that torments us. While obviously symbolic, I do think Jungians spend much of their time "chasing tulpas". 

It is why upon reading the Tao Teh Ching I was enamored with it's thought and words. It's so utterly brilliant and uniquely Eastern. It is, in many ways, the antithesis to the alchemical search: just be. 

But, we must also understand that there is value in it. I am cautious to recommend this book, but if you are to look into alchemy from Jung's perspective, I recommend Alchemy: An Introduction to the Symbolism and the Psychology by Marie Louis von-Franz. This will answer the question you posed succinctly. 

Nevertheless, there are two ways at looking at life: as a pilgrimage, or as Sisyphus. I agree with St. Augustine: we are all spiritual pilgrims here on this earth. 

The less you identify with yourself and the more you foster a relationship with God, the more you become yourself. 

Humans are, in many ways, are still animals.

No worries bro!

This passage reminded me of Aurelius:

"Enough talk about what a good man is. Just be one."

It also reminds me of something mentioned in regard to esotericism / hermeticism:

"Hermetic & esoteric religious traditions is like trying to go through the back door when the front door is wide open".

You can waste your entire life away looking within for something that doesn't exist. 

Incessant introspection won't necessarily make you a good man. 

Taoism is all about embodiment. We embody the Tao, we become in tune with it, as opposed to being wholly conscious of it. When you are conscious of something, it becomes intentional. 

For instance, in Taoism there is the concept of "High Integrity & Low Integrity". 

Somebody who has high integrity does not think about having integrity: he just embodies integrity without thinking about it. Low Integrity, however, requires conscious thought: "I am going to do this because it is good" instead of just being good, if you know what I mean. 

To follow the Tao is to follow the Way. It is the opposite of Sisyphus in many ways. Just drop the boulder. 

I know many people who spend their entire lives looking within. It can lead to a sort of narcissim. It misses the point in many ways. 

It's not that incessant introspection is entirely bad per se, like you said. It requires balance. 

But, "once you get the message, hang up the phone". 

Taking actions immeasurably more profound. 

Incessant introspection can lead to a sort of ego-inflation and megalomania. It reminds me of Plato's Allegory of the cave, and it's a criticism I have of Jung's psychology - an overidentification with the images themselves as integral aspects of ourselves (when that's not obviously the case). 

We look inside the cave at shadows when we all have the option to leave. Embodying the Tao and searching for the Way leads us out of the cave. 

"I was blind, and now I see" is what a blind man said after Jesus healed him. I also think he was able to then see "the Tao", which translated is "the Way".

I remember Jung once saying something like "you cannot individuate without being involved in the world and the affairs of others" (not an exact quote). 

But I do remember him saying this as well: "every individual is a link in a chain". 

Jung wasn't necessarily on board with ascetism, even recommending to his friend Victor White that perhaps he could follow his aspirations while not engaging in something as lonely as monasticism.

It makes sense, as in order to even project onto people we need to know them in the first place. 

The fact that projections land in the first place, to me, indicates that that which we project upon acts as an "imperfect vessel", and it is through working with others and ourselves that we reclaim these projections. 

The more you "individuate", which is just a long string of integration over the course of a life, the more you re-claim projections and are more easily able to connect with those around you and avoid archetypal inflation, which makes you more two-dimensional and ironically less of an individual. 

I hope that made sense lol

It is analysis because it focuses primarily on fantasies, dreams, affects, and such - as such, it is the "analysis of the soul". 

Modern psychology is usually understood as the study of the brain itself, it's makeup, and other similar things - which depth psychology / psychoanalysis does not touch.

Definitely it is still being used in modern depth psychotherapeutic practices (depth psychology & analytical psychology are just other terms for Jungian psychoanalysis). 

But again, most practicing psychologists (behavioral therapist, CBT, neuroscientists, and others) do not see the value in it.

It is rather niche, which is why I think Ahsan rightly saw that most people don't research it for it's depth psychological value.

You are right, historians, history of religions, and others do continue to study alchemy, but psychologists do not do so anymore (which i think was the original meaning of his question); they do so not for it's psychological information, but for it's historical relevancy. 

Jungians do still study alchemy, but they are psychoanalysts, not necessarily psychology proper.

It's a difficult questions for sure. 

It's obvious that one must consciously search for "enlightenment" or understanding or truth in the beginning. Nobody is born a sage. The search is integral towards reaching the goal. 

But, I think there comes a point when "once you get the message, hang up the phone". 

In other words, we can go chasing waterfalls because we like the thrill of the chase. Many learned men keep learning and learning not out of healthy curiosity, but because they are "searching for the truth" which never comes. The goal keeps growing further, the boulder never reaching the top of the hill. 

I say, just drop the boulder and begin following the Tao, drop the boulder and begin carrying your cross.

In the 19th century, a Tao sage remarked to a Western Christian who was visiting him (and I am paraphrasing):

"It has been over 2000 years since Laozi wrote the Tao Teh Ching. Ever since then, Taoism has split into hundreds of different schools and hundreds of alchemical sects. Where have we gone wrong, when the truth was simple: all is one, and all we need is 'The Way'..."

I think about it like this in many ways:

"The truth is simple, but the way is complex". 

And along the way we might have to do a ton of work and introspection and such. 

But my tweet was also in reference to Gnosticism. 

"Gnosis" is the inner search for God and the belief that inner knowledge leads to salvation. It is a lifelong goal and available for only the select few, those few "enlightened" individuals who have reached Gnosis. 

They stand on top of others due to their supposed inner knowledge and transcendence, when a sage realizes he is no better than his neighbor. I think our modern Western culture is, in many ways, unconsciously Gnostic. 'Whatifalthis' has a good video on his youtube channel about this. 

In short, it can lead to inflation I suppose. Here, I refer to Aurelius:

"Enough talk about what a good man is: just be one".

Easier said than done I know! And we should continue being learned men. But the Christian knows this: the fight has already been won.

Alchemy isn't researched by psychologists now because psychology split sometime in the mid-20th century. 

In the beginning there were the psychoanalysts, with the big three being Adler, Freud, & Jung. 

Out of the three, Adler is seen in the highest regard. Freud is second (for intellectuals love him but can't reconcile the failure of his dogmatic sexual unconscious), and the third is Jung because we was too much of a mystic. 

Even though Jung is far more profound in modern culture and society (and psychology, Jung coined the terms introverted, extroverted, complex, etc), he isn't talked about at all because of his interest in alchemy and religion. 

So, there are many Jungians today who study alchemy and such, but it's difficult to do so. Many, instead, study Jung's material itself to study alchemy.

Alchemical texts are inherently difficult to read, as they are coded in symbols so outsiders couldn't tell what they were doing. Jung was only able to read them due to his assistant / analyst Marie Louise von-Franz, who translated texts from Latin and Greek into German for Jung. 

So it's a difficult enterprise to study alchemy! 

And, modern psychologists don't study alchemy because they don't really see the immense value of dreams, fantasies, and affects of an individual. Rather, they focus more on the structure of the brain itself. It is why CBT therapy is incredibly popular.

Dante is right about the metaphysical reality of evil, but Milton is right about how evil manifests itself in a human. The former is theological, the latter is human-centric. It is why Milton's Satan has so much personality, and why evil itself is devoid of it.

It is only through the God-given creative spirit of mankind, which is fundamentally good, that gives evil it's color. 

Think of all of the wonderfully charismatic cult leaders. Think of the color of Fidel Castro. Think of all of the evil men who have duped the pious due to their charisma. 

It is the human that colors evil through his God-given creative gifts.

But, at the end of the day, when death falls upon those colorfully evil people, they will witness the true reality of evil devoid from the God-given creative spirit of every individual: cold, endless, repetition... like a turntable playing without a record, always looking for the music when there is none, and there never will be.

This is the fate of those without forgiveness.

So Milton is commenting on humanity, I imagine, while Dante is commenting on a true metaphysical reality.

I've thought for years that if we are able to control our algorithms in general it would make social media an incredibly more humane & beneficial experience. 

The fact that on Instagram I'm subjugated to reels, stories, and the explore page when in reality I usually only want to see what my friends are up to is very annoying. Having no option to remove these features sucks. And having a monkey brain, if I use these app I inevitably fall into their Pavlovian tricks.

Also the fact that I am unable to control what I see (the algorithm) on practically every social media is terrible. Why am I unable to tell Instagram reels to stop sending me a specific genre or type of content that I do not want to see for an endless number of reasons? I would 100% pay for this on other platforms if they offered it - just like some of us pay for Twitter.

The social media of the future, if it is not a dystopia, should be centered around user control of the experience & platform itself, as opposed to complete corporate control. 

I think Elon is setting the pace with Twitter, but there is more work to be done.

Marie Louise von-Franz on the "puer aeternus", a type of mother complex:

"In Don Juanism there is another typical form of this same disturbance. In this case, the image of the mother-the image of the perfect woman who will give everything to a man and who is without any shortcomings is sought in every woman. He is looking for a mother goddess, so that each time he is fascinated by a woman he has later to discover that she is an ordinary human being. 

Once he has been intimate with her the whole fascination vanishes and he turns away disappointed, only to project the image anew onto one woman after another. He eternally longs for the maternal woman who will enfold him in her arms and satisfy his every need. This is often accompanied by the romantic attitude of the adolescent. 

Generally great difficulty is experienced in adaptation to the social situation and, in some cases, there is a kind of false individualism, namely that, being something special, one has no need to adapt, for that would be impossible for such a hidden genius, and so on. In addition there is an arrogant attitude toward other people due to both an inferiority complex and false feelings of superiority. Such people also usually have great difficulty in finding the right kind of job, for whatever they find is never quite right or quite what they wanted. 

There is always "a hair in the soup." The woman also is never quite the right woman: she is nice as a girlfriend, but. There is always a "but" which prevents marriage or any kind of definite commitment.

This all leads to a form of neurosis which H.G. Baynes has described as the "provisional life," that is, the strange attitude and feeling that one is not yet in real life. For the time being one is doing this or that, but whether it is a woman or a job, it is not yet what is really wanted, and there is always the fantasy that sometime in the future the real thing will come about.

If this attitude is prolonged, it means a constant inner refusal to commit oneself to the moment. With this there is often, to a smaller or greater extent, a savior complex, or a Messiah complex, with the secret thought that one day one will be able to save the world; the last word in philosophy, or religion, or politics, or art, or something else, will be found."

Taken from Marie Louise von Franz's book The Problem of the Puer.

C.S. Lewis on Carl Jung:

"The presence of such a primordial image in the psycho-analytic process itself is, I think, the explanation of its popularity—for the same image is aroused by Freudian analysis too. In this respect psycho-analysis heals some of the wounds made by materialism. For the general effect of materialism is to give you, where you expected an indefinite depth of reality, a flat wall only a few inches away. Psycho-analysis offers you some kind of depth back again—lots of things hidden behind the wall. Hence those who have once tasted it feel that they are being robbed of something if we try to take it from them.

The emotional power of Jung’s essay [Mind and the Earth] is, as far as it goes, a proof that he is quite right in claiming that certain images, in whatever material they are embodied, have a strange power to excite the human mind. Every sentence he writes helps to prove this. At the same time we may be cautious about accepting his explanation, since there are some grounds for suspecting that the argument seems plausible not because of its real cogency but because of the powerful emotions it arouses. Has Jung, in fact, worked us into a state of mind in which almost anything, provided it was dim, remote, long buried, and mysterious, would seem (for the moment) an adequate explanation of the ‘leap in our blood’ which responds to great myth?

Let us look at the matter in cold prose. We want to know why certain images are exciting. Jung replies, ‘because they are ancient, because, in contemplating them, we are doing what our prehistoric ancestors did’. Now the idea that we are doing so is certainly exciting, as all ideas of antiquity are. But this idea is not necessarily entertained by the man in the moment of responding to a myth. He may not have read Jung’s theory; he may think that what he is contemplating is quite new: he may not raise the question of its age at all. Nevertheless he will respond. If Jung is right, then, it is not the idea of following our remote ancestors which produces the response but the mere fact of doing so, whether we are conscious of this fact or no. But there is no evidence that the actual reproduction of prehistoric behaviour, apart from the reflection that we are reproducing it, is at all exciting or impressive. We reproduce very ancient modes of behaviour in all our humblest animal operations. We are at one with our pre-Adamite sires when we scratch; and though I have no wish to underrate the pleasures of a good scratch, I think them very unlike those of a good poem.

No doubt even scratching may be made poetical if we reflect on the antiquity of the practice: but the pleasure we shall then get will not be the pleasure of scratching (the οἰκεία ἡδονή) but the pleasure of historico-poetical meditation. In the same way, I suggest, Jung has not explained the pleasure of entertaining primordial images but exhibited the pleasure of meditating on them and of entertaining, in the process, one particular primordial image, which itself needs explanation as much as any of the others. The idea that our sorrow is part of the world’s sorrow is, in certain moods, moving enough: the mere fact that lots of other people have had toothache does not make toothache less painful.

I have no answer to the question Jung has raised. I can only say— indulging once more in the same primordial image—that the mystery of primordial images is deeper, their origin more remote, their cave more hid, their fountain less accessible than those suspect who have yet dug deepest, sounded with the longest cord, or journeyed farthest in the wilderness—for why should I not be allowed to write in this vein as well as everyone else?"

Taken from "Psychoanalysis and Literary Criticism", found in his "Selected Literary Essays anthology".

He also comments on Freud. He was not a fan lol.

[St. Augustine on his conversion from Manicaenism to Christianity]:

"I still thought that is is not we who sin but some other nature that sins within us. It flattered my pride to think that I incurred no guilt and, when I did wrong, not to confess it...

I preferred to excuse myself and blame this unknown thing which was in me but not part of me. The truth, of course, was that it was all my own self, and my own impiety had divided me against myself.

My sin was all the more incurable because I did not think of myself a sinner". 

It seems as if, for many, we come to Christ when we recognize that our shadow is ours, that we ourselves sin, and that we are utterly helpless to correct the situation without help.

100%. Their diagnostic power is incredible.

It is one of the reasons I was "sold" on Jung's work.   

When I was in my first or second year of uni, I picked up a book called "The Problem of the Puer" by Jungian analyst Marie Louise von-Franz.

When I read the words on these pages, It was genuinely a difficult and wonderfully interesting experience. 

There, upon the pages written by an elderly Swiss German woman in the 70's, lied thoughts, affects, feelings, and peculiarities that I thought were my own and so original, but in reality were due in part to a complex. 

Because of this I have grown.

So, considering it's diagnostic power, I am inclined to to be sympathetic towards it, but, considering it's shadow of religiosity and the other "solutions", I am inclined to agree with you: it may worsen the human condition. 

I do not think it is cut and dry, but the key take away is to never become a Jungian.

I think it depends on the perspective. 

Now, we have nukes and bioweapons. These can obliterate humanity. It is one of the only reasons why many say today is "much better", for before, if anybody stepped out of line they weren't threatened with nuclear annihilation. 

In the times of the NT, however, girls were sold for pennies to wealthy Romans as sex slaves. People were burned alive in wicker statues. Crucifixtion, obviously, were a thing. 

But also, things just as cruel happen in cartels and terrorists groups. 

So it's all relative I imagine. 

If we are speaking of the West (& parts of the East, like Japan, etc.), then yes we are better off, but even then that's not guaranteed and readily obvious. 

Societal & familial relationships as well seem to be at their all-time worst in the West & in general as a consequence of social media, so we've lost a lot. How do we define better?

I think many think materially in this regard: "we have phones, medicine, TV, social media, refrigerators, life is great!" when also perhaps they are estranged from their parents and live a lonely life. Give me liberty or give me death is what comes to mind... do we have liberty? I think many would give up their material possessions for healthy social & familial relationships.

I have come to a somewhat similar conclusion from a different perspective, but the Nietzsche angle is brilliant and something I have considered myself. 

Jung loved Nietzsche. He has a whole seminar on his work (the Zarathustra lectures), and he would write to Nietzsche's sister to ask her questions about his personal life. 

Jung, too, had a theologian father. Jung, when he was a young man, said he "felt nothing" during his communion or something like that. 

Jung resonated with the "death of God" & his work, and he saw Nietzsche's end of his life psychosis, where he signed his letters as "the crucified one" or as "dionysius" was the "archetype of the Self possessing Nietzsche."

Now, consider this passage from the Red Book:

"[Jung] - So you want me to rule? From whence do you take the right for such a presumption?

[Philemon] - The right comes to me because I serve you and your calling. I could just as well say, you came first, but above all your calling comes first.

[Jung] - But what is my calling?

[Philemon] - The new religion and its proclamation."

Philemon is Jung's "personified soul", but it spoke to him in a vision as if it were conscious. 

In short, Jung became Zarathustra, a prophet of a semi-religion in which the "new God image" can re-emerge from the collective unconscious, primarily through the individuation of individual's (it is interesting to note that it is the Philemon Foundation that keeps, collects, and publishes Jung's work and related materials). 

Jung saw himself as doing what Nietzsche didn't have the strength to do. He calls his process of individuation as growing "from the fertile grounds of Christianity".

Essentially, I came to the conclusion that it is a secular humanist semi-solipsistic mystery Gnostic religion under the guise of psychology, and Jungian can ironically spend their entire life not realizing what they are worshiping. They are unconscious in many ways.

And thus I became disillusioned, but still interested. It's conflicting because obviously I resonate with many of his concepts. But I 100% am more careful now and in no way shape or form want to "practice Jungianism".

And thank you for your Ted talk, I hope you appreciate mine lol

"Scientists don’t know why stable matter (protons and neutrons) must constitute of exactly three quarks of different colour-charge (protons have two up-quarks whereas neutrons have two down-quarks.)

The quarks maintain their unity by continually exchanging colour charges. Thus, the quarks are always changing colour, while maintaining the rule that there must always be three different colours. 

It is reminiscent of the mutual indwelling that creates the unity of the Trinity."

~ Mats Winther

“Beware of practicing your piety before men in order to be seen by them; for then you will have no reward from your Father who is in heaven.

Thus, when you give alms, sound no trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, that they may be praised by men. Truly, I say to you, they have received their reward.

But when you give alms, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing, so that your alms may be in secret; and your Father who sees in secret will reward you.

And when you pray, you must not be like the hypocrites; for they love to stand and pray in the synagogues and at the street corners, that they may be seen by men.

"It is well-known to most people that narcissism is about excessive self-love, but what is perhaps less realized is that selfishness and disconnect as well as a lack of identity are also core aspects of this condition.

We can see quite obviously in the myth how selfish and therefore disconnected Narcissus became due to his tendency to spurn everyone in favor of himself. 

Further, although his reflected image was not substantial, I think the other side of this episode in the story should also be considered.

Paradoxically, Narcissus' reflection might also symbolize a sense of his true being, but in projected form; since he cannot see himself as he really is (he can only see himself in a one-sided, inflated way), his own self is forced to appear on the outside according to the law of conservation."

~ Dr. Joseph A. Talamo, "A Jungian Depth Perspective on OCD"

Your Spanish example is amazing. 

In Spanish, whenever we speak of death, we use the transient "estar" as opposed to the definitive "ser". "El está muerto", not "El es muerto". So, in the same way I say "está aquí" - in reference to my transient location, I say "está muerto". Ser is improper here because baked within the language itself is an entirely different way of perceiving the world that is inaccessible if you do not speak that language. There are no direct translations - only approximations. Each language serves as a different colored lens.

In English, we are not afforded such a luxury, and there is no differentiation between the two. Death is much more definitive. It is in part why Americans are so scared of it, I imagine

"I speak Spanish to God, Italian to women, French to men, and German to my horse." - Charles V

The same is true of religion. There is a phenomenology baked into each respective language & religion. Hindi has 72 ways to say the word "love", with varying contexts. English has one. So if I were to translate the word "love" into Hindi, it would be incredibly difficult to convey even what the hell I'm even talking about if I hadn't done years studying the language, it's cultural development, etc... the same thing is true of religions. 

And, while every religion is true, each religion has fundamental claims that vary (Islam & Christianity, for instance, have fundamentally different foundational claims) Religions are, in large, written & recorded by humans. So, in truth, every religion is false in some ways as well! But the fault lies not within God, but within the human. Christ tells us "beware of the scribes", and this is true - many scribes have altered the words of the prophets and of God. The same can be said of Buddhism as well. The Buddha, in reality, never meant to start a religion but rather a practice - it was his followers who created the religion, and thus Buddhists prayer to Bodhisattvas to help then reach Enlightenment (in a similar way to how we pray to Saints to intercede and ask God to help us; perhaps it is because they are also seeking God and tried to find Him in Buddha). 

So, then, which religion do I practice? It would be the definition of syncretism to pray the rosary and then attend a Hindu temple, if you see my point. It is a tricky subject.

Each religion is true in that they perceive the same reality we all do, but just like the example I gave with language, the manner in which that religion is expressed is an entire universe in and of itself. Again in language, a gendered noun gives the noun a more masculine and feminine connotation, and in the unconscious of the individual, they perpetually view that object as being masculine or feminine. Let's say in Language A, the word for bridge is gendered as feminine, while in Language B it is masculine. They are the same object reality, yet the language of the native speaker colors how they perceive that reality.

A good way to think about it is the Tower of Babble. Perhaps it was not only the universal language that was broken, but religion as well. 

So should we try and rebuild Babble? I find the moral of the story is no. So in the end, the truth is simple - just follow the Tao, the Truth, and the Life. The way, however, is complex.

Let me give you a bit of where I'm coming from in regard to my perspective.

Consider this passage from Christian mystic Simone Weil:

"Greece, Egypt, ancient India, the beauty of the world, the pure and  authentic reflection of this beauty in art and science...these things  have done as much as the visibly Christian ones to deliver me into  Christ's hands as his captive. I think I might even say more...

Each religion is alone true, that is to say, that at the moment we are  thinking of it we must bring as much attention to bear on it as if there  were nothing else ... A "synthesis" of religion implies a lower quality  of attention."

It is important, in my estimation, to understand exactly what we are talking about and to give the religion we are analyzing our full attention. As an anthropologist as well, this is a key facet of the practice. While one can make comparisons - for the truth is universal - we must first try to understand precisely what it is that each religion claims (i.e., their respective theologies). In doing so, we are better able to understand the spiritual truth that underlays them. 

For example, the conception of Hell is a "place without God - an absence from His presence". So not necessarily everybody in Hell is in the lake of torture, but it is Hell because you are separated from God's love. It is also, theologically, eternal.

And when we speak of Naraka - which iteration are we speaking of? The Hindu, Jain, or Buddhist iteration? Each have their own respective theological understanding.

Unlike in the Abraham conception of Hell, Naraka is not eternal. It is a process of purification. A more apt comparison would be the Christian Purgatory - a temporal place in which one's sins are cleansed before entering Heaven - although Purgatory is neutral while Naraka is more negative (but also, it is ultimately positive, for the end result is purification; in Hell, you never leave - it is eternal).

Each religion has a specific claim, and in order to properly understand their spiritual truths we must understand them on their level. Calling them all the same thing is reducing them to an archetype, and as C.G. Jung notes, archetypes are quite two-dimensional. Viewing them this way "implies a lower quality of attention", as Weil stated. 

In giving them their full attention, we are more able to grasp the immense beauty and complexity of each religions respective theological claims. 

Once we understand this, then comparisons can be made and we can bridge gaps between the religions - but we must first analyze their foundations before any construction can begin.

Christians are called to live with Christ as our example. As we know, Christ's harrowing of Hell is one of the greatest triumphs and mysteries of the Christian tradition. 

Thus, like a Bodhisattva, Christians are called to face Hell and suffering. Every Christian is meant to harrow our own Hell, both personal and collective.

It should also be noted that the Buddhist conception of Hell is far different than the Christian one. It is more reminiscent of Sheol or Hades - a sort of underworld as opposed to a place of torment, so a syncretism of these terms is misplaced and doesn't do justice to both religious traditions.

Bodhisattva's don't go to Hell - they go to Naraka. The original language is important, for languages are never perfectly translated and the connotation of Hell is far different than Nakara. 

Respecting the original language leads to a more nuanced understanding and respect of the Buddhist tradition.

It is quite typical for introverted feeling types to avoid conflict at all costs. 

They try to maintain harmony in any situation that may be contentious. As opposed to having difficult conversations that may serve to strengthen a friendship through their resolution, they avoid the conflict entirely to escape the contention. Often, they will cut off friendships early and suddenly, before they are able to blossom into something quite meaningful:

"The introverted feeling type... avoids imposing itself, preferring instead the peaceful harmony of isolation, which often leads others to assume an aloofness that may be mistaken for indifference"

Thinking types often come into conflict with introverted feeling types:

"The thinking type finds it particularly difficult to understand the feeling type… because the introverted feeling type is not inclined to explain itself, it will appear unreasonable and arbitrary to the thinking type.”

~ Carl Jung, Psychological Types (for both quotes)

I also wonder how much of this is cultural. I imagine that if this same study was done in a different time period, the results would be wildly different. 

For example, it seems that in Jewish communities intelligence is highly sought after.

I simply find it impossible to draw the conclusion from an evolutionary perspective that both men and women are hard-wired to select less intelligent mates, since intelligence is precisely what gives us our evolutionary edge. This is impossible to deny.

I find it easy to believe, however, that in our current cultural climate that this (selecting for low intelligence) would be the case.

The problem is that we will never be able to run studies on our per-society ancestors. Culture influences things like this to a stunning degree. 

For instance, I hardly imagine that both men and women in late 19th century France - during revolutions in philosophy, government, art (impressionism), and more - would prefer to choose dumber mates, for the culture at that time valued such a thing. 

So, this article doesn't necessarily challenge age-old evolutionary biology but rather highlights how culture impacts mating practices. 

We need some physical anthropologists to comment on this. The loss of inter-disciplinary communication in academia, I believe, leads to biased studies like this. 

So while the observation is right - that we are currently trending towards choosing dumber mates - the conclusion is ultimately jaded. 

It simply doesn't make sense from an evolutionary perspective that humans didn't select for intelligence.

"Social media" is a misnomer. A mutual of mine once called twitter a "text-based MMO" - a video game - and I think this is a good way to frame it.

What I touched upon in the post I quoted is that social interaction itself has become gamified through "social media". It is a misnomer because a more honest name would be "gamified social interaction".

If you knew this person irl, would he be able to unfollow you? Certainly not. To do that irl would require a direct confrontation and the absolution of the friendship - a visceral, emotional and physical affair.

Unless the interaction is taken off twitter and effort is put in to know the person genuinely, many interactions here are incredibly shallow. Most do not put in the effort because.... why? That won't help him win the game.

It is also often very hard for people to genuinely recognize and feel the other soul at the end of a handle. 

To you, I am simply "the cartographer", an anonymous avatar which I use to navigate this game we're playing.

But here I type, a physical person with a first, middle, and last name. I have a life, a family, hopes and dreams and struggles, and most importantly, a body in which other people can sense and interact with. 

Through our online interactions you get none of this. I'm just an avatar, a persona crafted for a myriad of reasons I do not even fully understand.

However, if you make this aspect conscious, it allows you to navigate "social media" better, because while it is a game, that is not all it is!

There are cool ass people out there. That is something I was shocked about. 

In spite of all of the limitations, it is still incredibly amazing to see how many people out there still have a soul and actually to somewhat recognize the light behind every handle. 

"Namaste" means "the light within me recognizes the light within you". Social media, however, is shrouded in darkness, leaving those to neglect such an obvious fact.

C.S. Lewis once said that one has a tremendous shift in consciousness when we recognize that every human being is immortal. I think this is true, and I think the enemy tries to make us all forget such a fact. 

So, friend, do not be weary! There are companions waiting to be met on the journey in your real life - not this gamified social platform. 

We cannot even begin to understand the true psychology of other people, especially on Twitter, so do not beat yourself up too much about this! 

In the end, it is best to be wise who you admire. We should not create false idols, for the only one who shall never fail us is God.

Twitter (and social media in general) is a social drug: the crack of collaboration. 

It has gamified and commodified the very essence of human consciousness - social interaction.

It is a slot machine of validation. 

We can be isolated from our neighbors and family but duped into feeling socially integrated due to social media.

Paradoxically, one can physically see 3 people in their day yet interact with thousands ethereally.

It can make one feel he is well-integrated when in reality he lives in social isolation.

Any social interaction that has no basis in physicality is only half of a social interaction, for I cannot discern much about one's character behind well-thought out Tweets and profile pictures. 

Everyone who searches for online validation carefully crafts their online persona in order to represent an ideal or a social archetype (there is always a grain of truth, but nevertheless, it is always curated towards algorithmic gain).

Greater is the man who pays no heed to such validation. If he truly cares not for it, then he may continue his work safely. Any following this man garners is a divine mandate. 

Otherwise, he is a trickster, crafting a meticulous illusion that blinds himself and his audience.

One should plant their feet firmly in the world and their neighboring society, for online social interactions are implicitly illusory, while those who you know in your real life live, breath, struggle, and celebrate with the reality of who you are.

As we know, nobody can exist without their bodies. 

Likes and retweets are a poor substitution for hand shakes, high-fives, hugs, and words of affirmation - and this is exactly what social media has commodified.

Japan and the Catholic church have a long history. There were more Catholics in Japan in the late 16th century than today. 

Catholicism became the main religion of Nagasaki at one point. It was such a strong force the shogun banned it, crucified Portuguese priests & Japanese Christians (as well as boiled them alive), and forced every Japanese to step on idols of Madonna & Child (a yearly practice known as 'fumi-e').

It didn't work though. Those that refused were murdered, but many who complied continued practicing in secret.

These Christians were known as the "Kakure Kirishitans" - the hidden Christians. They practiced Christianity for hundreds of years before its legalization.

So Christianity is not foreign to the Japanese. It was such a tremendous breath of fresh air to the highly legalistic, caste-based system of Japan (merchant -farmer - samurai - lord). It told women they need not to be concubines and to the samurai who their true Lord was. 

The church knows this. Anime has entertained Christian ideas and thought for years. A vast majority of irreligious Japan get married in a Christian chapel in white dress - a culturally Christian product.

So I suspect Luce is an attempt to continue this lineage.

I think what Thomas is trying to convey here is that many Western practitioners who think they are practicing Buddhism are not actually practicing Buddhism proper, but rather a Westernized version. 

I find it very interesting how Westerners always try and justify the religious traditions in the East through science, since science is a culturally Christian & Western invention. I do not think it is a fruitless task, but you get my point.

The East did not develop science because one of the hallmarks of a scientific endeavor is desire. Each scientist desires the truth or an outcome or a final endpoint... such tasks are fruitless in the eyes of the traditional East (not so much anymore due to globalism, but I digress).

So fruitless were these endeavors in their eyes that India itself neglected to record it's own history as they saw it as an illusion. And to the Taoist and Buddhist, science proper is beside the point in many ways.  

Nevertheless, many in the West extract the benefits from Buddhism yet neglect its more spiritual side, which does indeed involve prayer to Bodhisattva's (to aid one on their journey towards Enlightenment), a belief in an afterlife, and even a belief in a "messiah" (the Buddha 'Maitreya', who is to come in the eventual future when the Buddha's teachings are lost).

It is obvious that many Western practitioners of Buddhism do not believe this. They do the breath work and the meditation because the science bolsters it, as it "decreases levels of neuroticism and depression, improve neural networks, etc."... so they end up practicing Buddhism out of a desire to improve these metrics!  

They end up striving after the wind, never leaving the realm of rational science, for they continue to use a culturally Christian metric - science - to bolster the claims of another religion. It's quite a conundrum, really.  

So, as opposed to tarnishing the legacy of Eastern Buddhism, I think Thomas is trying to convey that many in the West who say they are practicing Buddhism really aren't practicing Buddhism, and they haven't genuinely began to embark on a true spiritual journey, hence "secular rationalism". 

I do think the title could be better. "The Rationalization of the Buddha" is quite tantalizing, but I don't think that is what he was getting at.  I hope you get my point, but I do think it is best to speak to others - especially in regard to spirituality - with a sort of softness, for it is easier to hear another when they aren't shouting.  

So while Thomas may not know much about Buddhism proper, he does know something about the "westernized, secular rationalist" edition of it, for that is the tradition he left when he became Orthodox, if I am not mistaken, so he is speaking on this specific cultural phenomenon that has been occurring in the West, I imagine.

"Such people make no claim to know what is really happening. They only know that when they do what Christ or their God directs them to do, they are experiencing reality... Myths are not lies, as they are often thought to be. Instead, they convey truths, albeit in a form that goes beyond our understanding."

~ C.S. Lewis

"The Phenomenology of Initiation", by Eliade:

"Initiation usually comprises a threefold revela-
tion: revelation of the sacred, of death, and of sex.

The child knows nothing of these experiences;
the initiate knows and assumes them, and incorporates them into his new personality. 

We must add that, if the novice dies to his infantile, profane, non-regenerate life to be reborn to a new, sanctified existence, he is also reborn to a mode of being that makes learning, knowledge, possible. 

The initiate is not only one newborn or resuscitated; he is a man who knows, who has learned the mysteries, who has had revelations that are metaphysical in nature. 

During his training in the bush he learns the sacred secrets: the myths that tell of the gods and the origin of the world, the true names of the gods,the role and origin of the ritual instruments employed in the initiation ceremonies (the bull-roarers, the flint knives for circumcision, etc.). Initiation is equivalent to a spiritual maturing.

And in the religious history of humanity we constantly find this theme: he who knows.

The ceremony everywhere begins with the separation of the candidate from his family and a period of retirement in the bush. Here already there is a symbol of earth; the forest, the jungle, darkness symbolize the beyond, the "infernal regions." 

In some places it is believed that a tiger comes and carries the candidates into the jungle on his back; the feline incarnates the mythical Ancestor, the master of the initiation, who conducts the boys to the underworld. Elsewhere the novice is believed to be swallowed by a monster. 

In the monster's belly there is cosmic night; it is the embryonic mode of existence, both on the cosmic plane and the lane of human life. In many places there is an initiatory hut in the bush. 

Here the young candidates undergo part of their ordeals and are instructed in the secret traditions of the tribe. Now, the initiatory hut symbolizes the maternal womb1'; the novice's symbolic death signifies a regression to the embryonic state. 

But this is not to be understood only in terms of human physiology but also in cosmological terms; the fetal state is equivalent to a temporary regression to the virtual, precosmic mode."

"For from within, out of the heart of man, come evil thoughts, fornication, theft, murder, adultery, coveting, wickedness, deceit, licentiousness, envy, slander, pride, foolishness. All these evil things come from within, and they defile a man." (Mark 7:21-23)

Jung, who disagreed with privatio boni,  has this to say on the manner:

“Evil, however, is no mere negation; it is a real factor in the world and the end results of human wickedness and human stupidity are measurable.”

I don't agree with Jung's ultimate conclusion, "that evil is the counter-pole of good, and this real polarity requires that evil should be placed as the opposite of good", but I do think it is useful to view evil as real, for it becomes tangible, for we can see how "the prince of darkness" works through the world and thwarts our every step.

In the end, the greatest trick the Devil ever pulled was...

I love Jung’s work but there’s definitely something sus about it. People worship his work and his philosophy of individuation has flaws.

He has bangers upon bangers for sure, but I am dubious of Jung’s intentions sometimes.

In the Red Book, Jung had a waking vision in which he was on the cross crucified, as if he were Christ, with Salome watching. 

He also said his “soul” spoke to him and told him to start a new religion (he was shocked, but nevertheless that’s pretty wild).The danger is self-worship & Jung worship, leading us to take ourselves too seriously and literally. 

In reality we’re not in control. 

This is why Jung’s Answer to Job was truly unfortunately misguided: Jung argues with God, implying that Job was more moral and conscious than God, thus obliging Yahweh to “apologize to us humans” through sacrificing himself as Christ. 

Of course, to Jung Christ is the ultimate symbol of “the Self”….

What are we doing here? That’s kinda absurd. 

That’s an obvious grift in my estimation and not the point of Job, which is that we are actually very puny and God is very mighty, and the we actually don’t have much control. This prevents us from partaking and experiencing life if taken too far.

Thus I am reminded of this verse from Isaiah: 

“How you are fallen from heaven, O Day Star, son of Dawn! How you are cut down to the ground, you who laid the nations low! 

You said in your heart, ‘I will ascend to heaven; above the stars of God I will set my throne on high; 

I will sit on the mount of assembly in the far north; I will ascend above the heights of the clouds, 

I will make myself like the Most High.”

The morning star is of course Lucifer. Considering Jung believes the godhead literally should include Satan, it seems to me as if something misguided Jung to positions that are almost too obviously evil.

Thus, it can be a “false-hold of the Anti-Christ”, which prevents us from having a very real relationship with the spirit of creation - God Himself.

The true start of the mystic & alchemical journey journey is realizing this falsehood and that there is, in fact, both an external and internal good & evil.

Christ is He who slays the Ouroboros.

Just my thoughts (which are still undergoing active development).

Interestingly, this is exactly the sentiment I agree with, but allow me to give some context:

Jung was adamantly opposed to the doctrine of “privatio boni”, Latin for “the privation of good”. 

“Privatio boni” is seen in what you say: “maladaptive traits aren’t’ evil, they are outcomes of feedback loops without awareness…”

Or, in other words, it is disregulation - a system that was once operating properly has entered into decay or unawareness.

Jung argued for a sort of dualism in regards to good and evil, using examples such as “hot and cold, above and below, light and dark” in order to convey evil’s metaphysical reality, which he then uses to place Satan (evil) into the Godhead (the Self), creating the quaternary model of the psyche and the Self.

However, physics has revealed that darkness quite literally is the absence of photons (light), and cold the absence of heat; this is “privation boni” - evil is the absence of good.

To take it back to the post, Jung once mentioned the example of the salamander to explain the Self. When the salamander loses it’s tail, there is some intrinsic archetype in the brain of the salamander that represents its wholeness - otherwise it wouldn’t be able to grow its tail without an intrinsic image of itself.

So using Jung’s own metaphor we have and example of how the Self regulates towards good itself.

“The Self” is our own image of wholeness, responsible for guiding us towards healing and awareness. How then could the Self intrinsically be evil - represented by Satan in the Godhead - if evil causes sickness, unawareness, and death?

The first thing I will say is that do not become a Jungian.

He is no savior and no saint. His work, while incredibly profound, has issues. 

To answer your question about Alchemy, Jung was interested in it because he saw it as a primitive form of a "psychology of the unconscious". 

The alchemists were "naive" as described by Jung and allowed the unconscious to manifest without judgement or alteration. What occurred psychically was recorded, giving us an "untainted" view of unconscious contents. However, Jung often projects his own psychology onto the Alchemists. 

Jung sees Alchemy as the "process of individuation manifested into reality", ie integrating unconscious archetypes into conscious understanding. He is not entirely wrong per se, but let me add very important context around his work:

I became concerned about Jung's work when I read parts of the Red Book actually. It is in many ways an evil book.

If you remember while listening, Jung mentions how during his visionary experiences his soul (Philemon) told him to start a new religion. Additionally, he had a vision of himself on the cross as Christ. The Red Book, of course, was the foundation of his most prolific work. 

In "Aion", Jung argues that Satan is just the shadow of God, implying that God is, in part, evil. Additionally, in "Answer to Job", Jung argues that God will re-incarnate himself as "the Anti-Christ". 

Does that not sound inherently wrong and evil? I think so. 

I mention all of this to save you some trouble before you get into Jung's work.

I, for the longest time, thought all I was studying was psychology “the mainstream didn’t get”. 

However, upon reading “Answer to Job” I realized something was wrong with the Jungian system as I understood it, and that what I was doing was not psychology but something else entirely.

Perhaps it is a sort of “spiritual philosophy”, but I am unsure. It is difficult to tell exactly what his work is at this point.

I do think there is legitimate psychology in his work. All of the “real” psychologists took his early work (which is why we still have use his terms) and left his mystical work behind (which includes but is not limited to “Aion, Liber Novus, Mysterium Coniunctionis”, etc).

One thing is for certain: he was correct in his assertion that there is much more to life than we can see. His unconscious theory, if it isn’t psychological (as it probably isn’t contained in the structure of the brain), is spiritual. Plato spoke of something similar, although he called the archetypes “the Forms” (think of a Platonic solid).

And the one thing I will say for certain is that if you practice Jung’s techniques, (such as dream analysis, journaling, active imagination, etc) you get a very, very real response.

So there is something substantial there. It is not psychology however. 

But the very fact that he treated patients for over 40 years and healed hundreds of neuroses (even those with psychosis) using his symbolic approach to the psyche substantiates to me that his system grasps something true.

What that is exactly I am unsure at this point.

Also, I find it interesting how you said he invented scientific terms for spiritual ideas. 

“Projection” is mentioned many times by Christ: “Why do you measure the speck in another man’s eye when there’s a plank in your own”, as well as “Judge not, lest you be judged”. There are many other examples.

Jung calling this “projection” abstracted the phenomena and allowed us to see it more clearly in the world, but it is inherently a spiritual phenomena.

I’ll be thinking about this more, thank you for your comment.

Find someone whose epistemology you respect

Your views will change over time so finding someone whose meta-level thinking is compatible is more important than their exact views at a given snapshot of time
